actions.shotDescription: A woman stands at the entrance of a brightly lit Tokyo alley (Omoide Yokocho) at night, introducing the video's topic.

params.text: WHAT NOT TO DO for your 1st trip IN TOKYO

actions.text: this is what not to do on your first trip to Tokyo

actions.shotDescription: A sped-up, 360-degree camera view of the bustling Shibuya Crossing at night.

actions.text: not all restaurants are built to accommodate long sessions of eating

actions.shotDescription: A chef's hands in blue gloves slice fresh tuna at a market stall.

actions.text: many small family owned restaurants can only fit a few customers at a time

actions.shotDescription: Top-down view of a bowl of tuna donburi (rice bowl).

actions.text: don't have a three hour dinner date or a lengthy catch up session with your friends

actions.shotDescription: A bowl of tsukemen noodles next to a bowl of dipping broth and a soft-boiled egg.

actions.text: and while making substitutions to your meal in your country might be more common

actions.shotDescription: A person lifts tsukemen noodles with chopsticks from a bowl.

actions.text: it's best in Japan if you just stick to what you're served

actions.shotDescription: Dipping the noodles into the rich broth.

actions.text: especially when language barriers make it really difficult to communicate

actions.shotDescription: Side profile of a man eating the noodles from the bowl.

actions.text: so instead find a restaurant that actually caters to your needs

actions.shotDescription: A Shinkansen (bullet train) pulls into a station platform.

actions.text: the next biggest thing is talking loudly on public transit

actions.shotDescription: Looking up at the iconic mirrored escalator entrance of Tokyu Plaza in Harajuku.

actions.text: save your conversations and phone calls for later

actions.shotDescription: POV shot looking down the kaleidoscopic, mirrored escalator.

actions.text: and this next one was so confusing for me as someone coming from Taiwan
the left side on escalators is actually for standing and the right side is for walking

actions.shotDescription: Top-down POV shot of two pairs of feet walking on a paved surface.

actions.text: so do not stop on the right side

actions.shotDescription: A sunny day on a busy shopping street in Tokyo with pedestrians.

actions.text: or you'll make it so inconvenient for everyone behind you

actions.shotDescription: A hand holds a freshly made strawberry crepe in a pink paper cone.

actions.text: for the public toilets here

actions.shotDescription: Following a person walking through a very crowded street.

actions.text: this was actually a nightmare for me when I first visited Japan

actions.shotDescription: A wide shot of the extremely crowded Takeshita Street in Harajuku.

actions.text: because of their squat toilets

actions.shotDescription: Walking through a narrow alley at night, illuminated by a string of yellow paper lanterns.

actions.text: but then again in Taiwan squat toilets are also normal
try not to panic when you actually see one because for many reasons... people actually prefer them compared to western toilets

actions.shotDescription: The entrance arch to Takeshita Street on a sunny day, with crowds of people.

actions.text: And last but not least, PDA is something that you won't find in Japan

actions.shotDescription: A 360-degree camera shot walking down a neon-lit street at night.

actions.text: cause PDA will actually make people uncomfortable here
the same goes for greeting your new Japanese friends

actions.shotDescription: Another 360-degree camera shot walking down a street with bright pink and purple neon signs.

actions.text: hugging and kissing as greetings are not part of their culture

actions.shotDescription: A final 360-degree camera shot walking down a street, looking up at large billboards.

actions.text: so please comment down below if you have any more tips on what not to do in Tokyo!
